text: We are seeing a case of a sixty two years old male with a lower thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma post neoadjuvant chemoradiation. The growth was starting at thirty five centimeters and there was a good response to neoadjuvant chemoradiation. Thoracoscopy was done in prone position with the ports mainly in the mid axillary line and in the mid scapular line as depicted.

The surgeon opens the mediastinal pleura to expose the azygos vein. Dissection is performed around the vein to encircle and ligate it, providing access to the esophagus. The vein is ligated below at its entry to the superior vena cava, clipped above, and divided in between, enhancing the exposure of the esophagus.

The surgeon identifies the bronchial artery arising from the intercostal artery behind the azygos vein. The mediastinal pleura is incised anterior to the azygos vein from above to below, encountering and sharply dissecting numerous direct esophageal branches from the descending thoracic aorta. The dissection progresses from above towards the carina and continues down to the hiatus.

The surgeon uses pneumodissection to separate loose areolar tissue, revealing the thoracic duct as a yellow structure located behind the azygos vein and posterior to the esophagus. Direct esophageal branches from the descending thoracic aorta are clearly visible.

The surgeon carefully avoids opening the left pleura while staying close to the esophagus. The mediastinal pleura is incised anterior to the esophagus from the carina to the crura. The inferior pulmonary ligament is divided between the right lung and the esophagus up to the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.

The surgeon carefully lifts and separates the esophagus from the pericardium, revealing the white caudal structure of the right vagus nerve running parallel. Care is taken to avoid dividing the nerve before it branches to the right bronchus. A plane is created circumferentially around the esophagus, away from the tumor, using gradual dissection. An umbilical tape is hooked around the esophagus, tied slightly tightly to allow movement for traction, aiding in further dissection.

The surgeon uses a slightly tight umbilical tape tied around the esophagus to provide traction for dissection. The descending thoracic aorta is visible, and its branches to the esophagus are carefully dissected and taken down using harmonic or vessel sealers, extending to the level of the crura.

The surgeon identifies the level of the esophageal growth and proceeds below it. The visible LOA structure is the gastroesophageal junction pad of fat, while the circular brown structure being dissected is the diaphragmatic crura. The dissection has reached the maximum level below the growth.

The surgeon carefully dissects the esophagus away from the left bronchus and trachea, with visible inflating and collapsing movements of these structures. The dissection proceeds supra-carinal, using Ligasure to avoid injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The bronchial artery arising from the intercostal artery is taken down for better exposure, and loose fibers between the trachea and esophagus are sharply dissected.

The surgeon dissects above the left bronchus, carefully avoiding parallel structures such as the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The dissection proceeds from the crura to the thoracic inlet, marked by the inferior thyroid artery. Subcarinal lymphadenectomy is performed, with care taken to preserve small arterial tributaries visible near the whitish bronchial wall and red vascular structures, using a vessel sealer instead of cautery.

The surgeon performs a two-field lymphadenectomy, including dissection around the left recurrent laryngeal nerve. The vagus nerve is carefully pushed aside to preserve its branch to the left bronchus. A lymph node is dissected at the level of the bronchus, with the fibrillating atria visible in the background.

The surgeon focuses on the supracarinal region, preserving branches to the trachea from the arch or descending aorta. The superior pulmonary vein is visible as a whitish structure below, while the arch of the aorta curves above. Dissection occurs in the aortopulmonary window, using blunt or sharp dissection with scissors, and short bursts of energy from harmonic or ligature devices to avoid injury to structures parallel to the esophagus.

The surgeon sharply divides the branches of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve to the esophagus and pushes them away. The nerve is seen hooking around the arch of the aorta and running parallel to the trachea and esophagus. The inferior thyroid artery is identified as a white structure, and lymph nodes along this artery are dissected. The left vagus nerve is visible hooking around the arch of the aorta, giving rise to the left recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon focuses on the right side of the trachea, dissecting the mediastinal pleura away from the right vagus nerve. The right paratracheal lymph nodes are carefully dissected using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, including scissors and short bursts of vessel sealer. The superior vena cava is visible below, and the pleura's attachment to the superior part of the esophagus is taken down. The right vagus nerve and the right recurrent laryngeal nerve are identified, with branches to the esophagus being divided. The lymphofatty tissues are removed from both nerves, completing the right-sided paratracheal lymphadenectomy. The right subclavian artery is also visible, with the recurrent laryngeal nerve traced from the right vagus.

All lymph nodes are removed, and an intercostal drain (ICD) is introduced as the lung is inflated under direct vision. Laparoscopy is performed with the patient in the French position, using standard upper abdomen ports. The left lobe of the liver is retracted with a toothed self-locking retractor. The gastrocolic ligament is dissected away from the arcade, progressing from the right gastroepiploic artery to the fundus.

The surgeon approaches the spleen, where the arcade ends and the short gastric vessels originate. To prevent injury to the stomach, a combination of posterior and lateral dissection is performed to release the short gastric vessels. The vessel sealer is used to minimize lateral injury, while the fundus is carefully separated from surrounding structures.

The surgeon pulls down the esophagus, performing dissection superiorly and towards the left side to separate it from the crura. The phrenoesophageal membrane is incised, and the mediastinum is visible. Posteriorly, the tumor involving some crural structures is taken down. Attention shifts to the right side, where the right crura is separated from the esophagus, completing circumferential dissection of the gastroesophageal junction. Small attachments in the gastrocolic ligament on the right side are also removed.

The surgeon identifies the vertical structure as the right gastroepiploic artery, which is preserved. The omental attachments between the duodenum and colon are dissected, facilitating the movement of the gastric conduit towards the midline. The right gastrointestinal arcade is taken down, and following lymphadenectomy, the left gastric artery is ligated. The gastric conduit is then prepared using an open technique.
